rulings.Case Title: SALMA KHAN MD VS. HEALTH SYSTEMS DEPARTMENT, SAN MATEO COUNTY, et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn To Compel Pltf'S Supplemental Responses To For Interoggs
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, January 21, 2014, Line 17, DEFENDANT COUNTY OF SAN MATEO Mtn To Compel Pltf'S Supplemental Responses To For Interoggs Granted. Within 20 days, Plaintiff shall serve verified supplemental responses to Form Interrogatories, nos. 202.1, 204.3, 204.6 and 208.1, 210.2, 210.3, 210.5, 212.4, 212.5 and produce responsive documents. Monetary sanctions of $ 1,000.00 are awarded in favor of Defendant Health Systems Department, San Mateo County and against Plaintiff Salma Khan, MD to be paid within 30 days of service of notice of entry of order. For Form Interrogatories nos. 202.1, 204.3, 204.6 and 208.1, Plaintiff shall set forth dates, identities of individuals to which she makes reference, and specifically identifying documents referred to in such responses. To the extent that plaintiff is unable to provide such information as to dates, individuals in connection with any of the special interrogatories or documents she should so state the reasons why she is unable to supply the information and, without a showing of good cause, will be precluded from relying upon the allegations of that particular form interrogatory at the time of trial. For Form Interrogatories nos. 210.2, 210.3, 210.5, 212.4, 212.5, Plaintiff shall state with specificity the amount of her claim for loss of benefits, income and earning capacity; details of all subsequent employment both as to identity of employer(s), dates of employment and payments received. In this regard, Plaintiff shall produce all documents that reflect such payments. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ehg

rulings.Case Title: LINDSTROM & SON BUILDERS VS. CANDLESTICK VIEW HOMEOWNERS'
rulings.Calendar Matter: PETITION TO VACATE ARBITRATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, January 21, 2014, Line 3, RESPONDENT CANDLESTICK VIEW HOMEOWNERS' PETITION TO VACATE ARBITRATION Denied. (CCP § 1286.2(a)(3) & (4).) Petitioner's objections to Baugh Declaration are sustained as to objection nos. 1 - 6 and overruled objection nos. 7 - 9, 10. Petitioner's objections to Bizieff Declaration are sustained. Respondent has not presented sufficient evidence to demonstrate that its rights were substantially prejudiced by misconduct of a neutral arbitrator or that the arbitrator exceeded his powers and the award cannot be corrected without affecting the merits of the decision upon the controversy submitted. (CCP § 1286.2(a)(3) & (4).) The declaration of Andrew M. Baugh does not demonstrate that the Arbitrator affirmatively ordered production of the RAA Reports during the arbitration. The declaration of Gordon M. Bizieff is insufficient to demonstrate that the arbitration award violates a public policy. The introduction of the declaration of Daniel L Rottinghaur in response to objections is improper and not considered by the Court. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ehg

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM Y. MOORES MD VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA, DIVISION OF MEDICAL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, January 21, 2014, Line 4, DEFENDANT LINDA WHITNEY, SHARON LEVINE, M.D., MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA, A PUBLIC ENTITY MOTION TO STRIKE 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT This motion is off calendar as moot in light of the Court's ruling on Defendant's demurrer. =302/ehg

rulings.Case Title: WILLIAM Y. MOORES MD VS. MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA, DIVISION OF MEDICAL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, January 21, 2014, Line 4, DEFENDANT LINDA WHITNEY, SHARON LEVINE, M.D., MEDICAL BOARD OF CALIFORNIA, A PUBLIC ENTITY DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT Defendant's demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. Plaintiff has not, and cannot, show the enactments alleged are intended to protect against the kind of risk of injury suffered by Plaintiff. (See Government Code 815.6; State of California v. Superior Court (Perry) (1984) 150 Cal.App.3d 848, 854.)) Under Cal. Code of Business & Professions § 2001.1, protection of the public is the highest priority for Defendant. Even if Defendant failed to provide a written response to the January 25, 2005 inquiry into Plaintiff's probationary status, Plaintiff cannot show that this was the proximate cause of his damages. (See Second Amended Complaint para. 84, Exh. 28; Perry, supra.) Additionally, Plaintiff's request to adopt the proposed order of the Administrative Law Judge through the present action is improper, as the appropriate remedy should be sought by a petition for a writ of mandate. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ehg

rulings.Case Title: DIGITAL REALTY TRUST, LP, A MARYLAND LIMITED et al VS. CAPSTAR COMMERCIAL REAL ESTATE SERVICES, LTD., A et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, January 21, 2014, Line 6, DEFENDANT CASSIDY TURLEY, INC., A MISSOURI CORPORATION, CASSIDY TURLEY COMMERCIAL REAL ESTATE SERVICES,, CAPSTAR COMMERCIAL REAL ESTATE SERVICES, LTD., A, CCRES, LTD., A TEXAS LIMITED PARTNERSHIP MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Denied. When a defendant seeks summary judgment based on an affirmative defense, the defendant must present evidence that, if uncontradicted, "would constitute a preponderance of the evidence" that the affirmative defense can be established. (Kids' Universe v. In2Labs (2002) 95 Cal.App.4th 870, 879.) The evidence submitted does not establish, by the preponderance of the evidence, that Plaintiffs had a "suspicion of wrongdoing." (See Jolly v. Eli Lilly & Co. (1988) 44 Cal.3d 1103, 1111.) Rather, the evidence shows that Plaintiffs had difficulties getting paperwork from Krahl Construction in a timely fashion and that Krahl Construction made errors on occasion on the paperwork it did submit. (See Evidence in support of Undisputed Fact ("UF") nos. 26-44.) Alternatively, even if Defendants had met their burden, Plaintiffs have submitted evidence that establishes a triable issue of material fact as to whether Plaintiffs had a suspicion of wrongdoing and therefore inquiry notice, or whether Tom Stratford's inquiries were to fulfill his "¿to push for timely and complete paperwork." (Declaration of John Thomas Stratford, 8-10.) Defendant met its burden of showing that Defendant Cassidy Turley, Inc. ("Cassidy") did not assume all the liabilities of Defendant Capstar Commercial Real Estate Services, Ltd. ("Capstar"). The burden then shifted to Plaintiffs. Plaintiffs met their burden of showing a triable issue of material fact exists as to whether a de facto merger occurred. Defendants are correct that Franklin v. USX Corp. stands for the proposition that when there is a cash purchase of assets, adequate consideration is the critical factor in determining whether a de facto merger has occurred. ((2001) 87 Cal.App.4th 615, 621.) However, as discussed in Franklin, where assets are purchased with stock, a de facto merger can be found. (Id. at 626.) Here, the evidence shows that Cassidy paid both stock and cash for virtually all of Capstar's assets. (UF no. 71; Troughton Decl., Ex. 5, Depo of Florent, 51:10-16.) Further, the cash was not paid to the entity itself, but directly to the limited partners of Capstar, outside of the reach of any potential unsecured creditors. (Troughton Decl., Ex. 5, Depo of Florent, 34:5-14; 59:21-25.) Therefore, there was not a cash purchase of assets made directly to the entity, as in Franklin. As such, there is a triable issue of material fact as to whether a de facto merger took place. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ehg

rulings.Case Title: HELGA KATRINA HORNER VS. DARIN GEISE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Compelling Deft Darin Geise Dba Coup D'Etat To Provide Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set One, Numbers 1,2,3,4 And 5; Proof Of Service
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, January 21, 2014, line 4 - PLAINTIFF HELGA HORNER and JOHN TOBELER's Motion For An Order Compelling Defendant Darin Geise Dba Coup D'Etat To Provide Further Responses To Request For Production Of Documents, Set One, Numbers 1,2,3,4 And 5, Pro Tem Judge Nils Rosenquest, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. Parties are to meet and confer on the following issues: (1) Responsive materials to relevant form interrogatories, (2) definition of the term, "incident" under the standard form interrogatories with respect to the allegations in the first and now second amended complaints, (3) asserted defects in privilege logs, and (4) terms of a suitable protective order for privileged and financial documents. The Court notes: (1) it has looked through the voluminous exhibits but has not located a copy of the referenced form interrogatories and the response to those interrogatories. Lacking that information, the Court has no basis upon which to rule with respect to Request Number 1. Plaintiff is requested to submit those documents (and those documents only) as a supplementary exhibit by email to the pro tem prior to the hearing [email address: nrosenquest@earthlink.net]. (2) At the hearing, the parties are requested to address the privacy issues (necessity vs. privacy interest) with respect to each category of documents requested and the terms of a suitable protective order. =(302/JPT)

Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. At the hearing, the parties are to address the privacy concerns as to each category of documents to which the privilege has been asserted, recognizing that certain classes of documents (such as tax returns) obtain higher degrees of protection than other types documents (financial statements that contain publicly available information). =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT VS. JDS BUILDERS GROUP, INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Tuesday, January 21, 2014, Line 11, CROSS DEFENDANT LEVY DESIGN PARTNERS, INC. DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT Cross-Defendant Levy Design Partners, Inc.'s ("LDS") demurrer to JDS Builders Group's ("JDS") Cross-Complaint is overruled in part and sustained in part. As to the second and third causes of action, the demurrer is overruled as LDS has not cited to any applicable authority demonstrating that a duty does not exist as a matter of law. As to the fifth cause of action, the demurrer is overruled as JDS has alleged sufficient facts to constitute a cause of action for declaratory relief. As to the fourth cause of action, the demurrer is sustained. JDS is given 10 days leave to plead facts, if it can do so in good faith, with particularity to meet the heightened pleading standard for negligent misrepresentation. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/ehg

Cross-Complainant JDS Builders Group has pled sufficient facts under the "tort of another" doctrine, which establishes grounds to recover attorney's fees. (See Prentice v. North Am.
